section.description: This section provides information about data that are re-used in the context of undertaking research.
section.title: Reused Data

section.description:
In general terms, your research data should be 'FAIR', that is findable, accessible, interoperable and re-usable. These principles precede implementation choices and do not necessarily suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution. This template is not intended as a strict technical implementation of the FAIR principles, it is rather inspired by FAIR as a general concept. Achieving FAIR data relies equally on the choices you make throughout a research process, but also on providers whose services enable FAIRness of data to be met. 
Learn more about the FAIR principles: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4792175/ 
Learn how you can comply with FAIR principles: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-make-your-data-fair
section.title: FAIR Data

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: Will you use metadata to describe the data?
fieldSet.description: Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. Descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its title, author/creator, abstract, keywords. Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

labelData.label: URL where metadata can be found
Name of repository, database, platform that holds the data

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Location describing the used metadata schema
Will your metadata use standardised vocabularies?
fieldSet.description: Standardised vocabularies enable greater interoperability across systems. There are generic and discipline specific metadata standards which have been compiled so as to contain vital information that enable exchange while taking into consideration specificities of work and demands in different disciplines, even in different areas of work within a discipline.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may browse the Metadata Standards Catalog here: http://rd-alliance.github.io/metadata-directory/

labelData.label: URL where the vocabularies of the data can be found
Briefly describe selected vocabularies application for your needs

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Description of used vocabularies
Will you make the metadata available free-of-charge?
fieldSet.description: You could offer your metadata records free of charge or assign them small fees. However, charging for metadata sharing would limit access and use. Please check your funder’s policy before deciding how and when to share metadata.

fieldSet.title: Will your metadata be harvestable?
fieldSet.description: Usually, this is facilitated by services that apply standard protocols for information exchange. For example, in repositories, metadata exchange is supported by the OAI-PMH protocol.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find more information about trustworthy repositories: https://www.openaire.eu/find-trustworthy-data-repository

fieldSet.title: Will you use naming conventions for your data?
fieldSet.description: Naming conventions used throughout the management process, such as file names or data entities, should be clearly described in order to make data understandable by other researchers who may reuse them after the project ends.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Check out some examples of file naming best practices: http://tiny.cc/op8lcz

labelData.label: URL where this information can be found
Name of the conventions used for the data

fieldSet.title: Please provide more details and examples on used naming conversions
Will you provide clear version numbers for your data?
fieldSet.description: Versioning may be a built-in functionality, subject to an instrument/service/tool that has been used for data processing and analysis which automatically produces versioned data. In other occasions, you will need to keep track and adapt to a versioning mechanism for your data. Here you should state how versioning takes place.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about how to handle and arrange different versions: https://www.openaire.eu/raw-data-backup-and-versioning

fieldSet.title: Will you provide persistent identifiers for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Persistent and/or Permanent Identifiers (PIDs) uniquely identify objects, people, organisations and activities and can ensure that the scientific output is accessible even when the URL of the website has changed. PIDs can be assigned to research outputs including publications, data and software/code. PIDs can also be assigned to researchers, samples, organisations and projects. A PID may be connected to a metadata record describing an item rather than the item itself. PIDs are usually provided by data repositories and other deposit platforms. Re3data (https://www.re3data.org/) includes tags to show which platforms that it indexes assign PIDs to their content.

fieldSet.title: Persistent identifiers
fieldSet.description: A persistent identifier or PID is a long-lasting reference to a resource. Whatever resource it refers to, the primary purpose of the PID is to provide the information required to reliably identify, verify and locate it. A PID may be connected to a set of metadata describing an item rather than to the item itself.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Learn more about PIDs and how to use them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-can-identifiers-improve-the-dissemination-of-your-research-outputs

fieldSet.title: Will you provide searchable metadata for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Searchable metadata are metadata indexed by search engines and are identifiable by web crawlers.

selectData.label: Metadata are indexed in/ by a

fieldSet.title: What services will you use to provide searchable metadata?

labelData.label: URL of the services providing searchable metadata
Name of the services used for providing data with searchable metadata

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Name for the used searchable metadata
Will you use standardised formats for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Different types of data are acquired, processed and stored (preserved and/or archived) in different ways and can be discipline specific. Many proprietary file formats are “containers” for standard file formats. By packaging them into these containers, a software and/or hardware developer can provide additional functionality, usually by streamlining a process, to analyse data acquired on their platform. However, this has the negative consequence of making these data less interoperable.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find some best practices for file formats: https://library.stanford.edu/research/data-management-services/data-best-practices/best-practices-file-formats

fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information about preserving data in appropriate formats: https://www.openaire.eu/data-formats-preservation-guide

fieldSet.title: Are the file formats you will use open?
fieldSet.description: Open file formats provide the software specifications to anyone for free and enable re-use. Examples: .png, .xml, .rtf, .wav

fieldSet.title: Please provide more details if data are not in an open format.
fieldSet.description: Closed file formats are intertwined with proprietary formats that either do not provide access to software specification as opposed to open or despite the fact that they do, they limit re-use by others. Examples: .doc, .jpeg, .mp3

section.description: Data are findable when described with metadata and vocabularies in a standardized way, assigned a Persistent Identifiers (PIDs) and are registered or indexed in a searchable resource.
section.title: Making data findable, including provisions for metadata

fieldSet.title: Are there ethical or legal issues that can impact sharing the described data?
fieldSet.description: It is possible that data are sensitive, such as personal or confidential, and hence they can not be shared directly to others. For those types of data, specific processes need to be made in order to ensure their reliable exchange in the short and long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about sensitive data and how to process them: https://www.openaire.eu/sensitive-data-guide

fieldSet.title: Will the described data be openly accessible?

fieldSet.title: How will the data be made available?
fieldSet.description: A data repository, otherwise known as a data archive, is an online collection of datasets that are described and classified in a standard way that makes data discovery and retrieval easier to be performed by both humans and machines.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information on the processes, you can have a look at https://www.openaire.eu/rdm-handbook.

fieldSet.title: Are there any methods or tools required to access the described data?
fieldSet.description: Describe processes needed to query and access data. These might be relevant to SPARQL access points, visualizer software, download, etc. Data access controls, such as passwords or firewalls, should be used to limit access to confidential data and protect them from unauthorised changes.

fieldSet.title: Will you also make auxiliary data that may be of interest to researchers available?
fieldSet.description: Auxiliary data are supplementary to the product generation data; data that come from an external source and not from direct instrument measures, but are used in the context of a study.

section.description: Not all data can be made publicly open, hence data can be FAIR but not open, or open but not FAIR or both FAIR and open. Data are accessible when uploaded in a data repository and retrieved by their PIDs. When data can not be shared openly, metadata should be provided (even when the data are no longer available). In the case of sensitive or personal data, anonymization or pseudonymization and specific access rights can be applied. Where accessing data requires the use of complementary methods or tools, such procedures should be documented.
section.title: Making data openly accessible

fieldSet.title: Will you use a controlled vocabulary for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Controlled vocabularies provide standard terminology as opposed to keywords or tags used to classify information. Examples: taxonomies, ontologies, thesauri

fieldSet.title: Will you provide a mapping to more commonly used ontologies?

section.description: Data are interoperable, meaning they can be easily understood and shared with other platforms and systems, when they are created using standard vocabularies and include references to other data and metadata.
section.title: Making data interoperable

Please specify how long after the project has ended the data will be made available
fieldSet.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.

fieldSet.title: Please describe the reason the desribed data will not be made available
What internationally recognised licence(s) will you use for the described data?
fieldSet.description: There are a number of licenses that can be assigned to your research outputs. There are different licenses for software and for publications and data. For the latter, a common practice is the use of Creative Commons licenses which are machine readable.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
You may learn more about how to choose a license here: https://www.openaire.eu/how-do-i-license-my-research-data
Additionally, you may use the License Clearance Tool (LCT) to establish the proper open license required for the creation of a new (or synthetic) dataset, media, software, etc. or for the re-use of existing unlicensed content.. Access the tool here: https://lct.ni4os.eu/lct/login

fieldSet.title: Do you have documented procedures for quality assurance of the described data?
fieldSet.description: Quality assurance procedures are closely related to RDM and are meant to validate that data are clean (no duplicates or inconsistencies), error-free, well structured and represented in analysis. Quality assurance checks can be facilitated by the use of tools or scripts.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To familiarise yourself with the process, you may find a checklist here: https://www.eur.nl/sites/corporate/files/2017-11/Checklist_QA_QC_RDM_0_2_MD_20150129.pdf

description.description:
SubTask 3.2.2
Spatial surveys on covariance between microbial diversity and MNP distribution

Datasets name:
Variability in microbial community composition in soils with varying microplastic concentrations

Dataset description:

Tabular data of values of molecular markers reflecting microbial community composition (with metadata including variable labels, code labels and definition of missing value, experimental design description). 

Data is collected from the 39 soil samples of spatial surveys in European regions (Germany, Spain, Finland and Czech Republic). Each soil sample was treated with conventional and biodegradable microplastics (two different concentrations), and each soil was incubated in the laboratorial conditions. The bacterial V4-V5 region of the 16S rRNA gene was amplified using primers 515F-926R, and the fungal ITS genomic region was amplified using the fITS7 and the ITS4 primers. After the purification, the amplicon library was sequenced on an Illumina MiSeq platform. The bacterial taxonomy of each amplicon sequence variant (ASV) was assigned using the database. The alpha diversity indices and relative abundances of bacterial and fungal communities were analyzed, and the beta diversity was visualized using Bray-Curtis distances.

1.    PAPILLONS European Spatial Survey: Microbial community in relation to microplastic load in European field soils

DOI and link in Zenodo:
Sequence data will be uploaded in NCBI, other data in Zenodo

Dataset current status: 
The dataset is currently completed in proportion of 60 percent
The expected delivery date of the corresponding dataset on PAPILLONS Zenodo Community and NCBI is June 2024.
description.label: SubTask 3.2.2 - Variability in microbial community composition in soils with varying microplastic concentrations

fieldSet.title: What is the purpose of the data collection/generation and its relation to the objectives of the project?
fieldSet.description: Data collection is usually at the beginning stages of research data management lifecycles to set the background of what is needed (data generation), what is already there (data reuse) and how to best use it to fulfill the project’s objectives (why). Here you may add information about the scope and objectives of your data collection process.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find a comparison of different data management lifecycles here: Plale, Beth & Kouper, Inna. (2017). The Centrality of Data: Data Lifecycle and Data Pipelines. 10.1016/B978-0-12-809715-1.00004-3.

options.value: observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: What are the types of the described generated/collected data?
fieldSet.description: The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. Primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. Secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information, have a look at: https://data.library.virginia.edu/data-management/plan/format-types/

fieldSet.title: What are the formats of the described generated/collected data?
fieldSet.description: Data may be found in different formats. Usually, outputs of specific instruments/ services/ tools where data are generated and/ or processed have a default way of exposing their outputs following specific standards. Most common distinctions between formats are proprietary and non proprietary formats, because they may impact data access in the long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information about formats, have a look at: https://www.ukdataservice.ac.uk/manage-data/format/recommended-formats

fieldSet.title: What is the origin of the described data?
What is the expected size of the described data?
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

fieldSet.title: To whom might it be useful ('data utility')?
fieldSet.description: Data generated or reused in the project can be useful for a number of stakeholders and third parties. Think about the target audience of your research, but also about possible third parties who could further exploit this data even after the project ends.

section.description: Hint: This section is usually associated with detailed activities of the project. If you are writing the DMP at a primary stage of the research process (i.e. first 6 months of the research project), think about the main activities that will be performed within Work Packages (WPs) and break them down so as to fit into a research data management lifecycle. Add new dataset description to describe different datasets or types of datasets that will be produced or re-used during the project.
section.title: Data Summary

options.value: To compare and combine with other data

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using the described data and how?
fieldSet.description: Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc. 

Learn more: https://www.openaire.eu/can-i-reuse-someone-else-research-data

fieldSet.title: Where do the described data reside?
fieldSet.description: Select the repository/ies where data are hosted.

fieldSet.title: Which data will be re-used?
fieldSet.description: Select the datasets that will be reused. Make sure that you select the correct filenames and versions!

section.description: This section provides information about data that are re-used in the context of undertaking research.
section.title: Reused Data

section.description:
In general terms, your research data should be 'FAIR', that is findable, accessible, interoperable and re-usable. These principles precede implementation choices and do not necessarily suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution. This template is not intended as a strict technical implementation of the FAIR principles, it is rather inspired by FAIR as a general concept. Achieving FAIR data relies equally on the choices you make throughout a research process, but also on providers whose services enable FAIRness of data to be met. 
Learn more about the FAIR principles: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4792175/ 
Learn how you can comply with FAIR principles: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-make-your-data-fair
section.title: FAIR Data

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: Will you use metadata to describe the data?
fieldSet.description: Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. Descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its title, author/creator, abstract, keywords. Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

labelData.label: URL where metadata can be found
Name of repository, database, platform that holds the data

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Location describing the used metadata schema
Will your metadata use standardised vocabularies?
fieldSet.description: Standardised vocabularies enable greater interoperability across systems. There are generic and discipline specific metadata standards which have been compiled so as to contain vital information that enable exchange while taking into consideration specificities of work and demands in different disciplines, even in different areas of work within a discipline.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may browse the Metadata Standards Catalog here: http://rd-alliance.github.io/metadata-directory/

labelData.label: URL where the vocabularies of the data can be found
Briefly describe selected vocabularies application for your needs

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Description of used vocabularies
Will you make the metadata available free-of-charge?
fieldSet.description: You could offer your metadata records free of charge or assign them small fees. However, charging for metadata sharing would limit access and use. Please check your funder’s policy before deciding how and when to share metadata.

fieldSet.title: Will your metadata be harvestable?
fieldSet.description: Usually, this is facilitated by services that apply standard protocols for information exchange. For example, in repositories, metadata exchange is supported by the OAI-PMH protocol.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find more information about trustworthy repositories: https://www.openaire.eu/find-trustworthy-data-repository

fieldSet.title: Will you use naming conventions for your data?
fieldSet.description: Naming conventions used throughout the management process, such as file names or data entities, should be clearly described in order to make data understandable by other researchers who may reuse them after the project ends.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Check out some examples of file naming best practices: http://tiny.cc/op8lcz

labelData.label: URL where this information can be found
Name of the conventions used for the data

fieldSet.title: Please provide more details and examples on used naming conversions
Will you provide clear version numbers for your data?
fieldSet.description: Versioning may be a built-in functionality, subject to an instrument/service/tool that has been used for data processing and analysis which automatically produces versioned data. In other occasions, you will need to keep track and adapt to a versioning mechanism for your data. Here you should state how versioning takes place.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about how to handle and arrange different versions: https://www.openaire.eu/raw-data-backup-and-versioning

fieldSet.title: Will you provide persistent identifiers for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Persistent and/or Permanent Identifiers (PIDs) uniquely identify objects, people, organisations and activities and can ensure that the scientific output is accessible even when the URL of the website has changed. PIDs can be assigned to research outputs including publications, data and software/code. PIDs can also be assigned to researchers, samples, organisations and projects. A PID may be connected to a metadata record describing an item rather than the item itself. PIDs are usually provided by data repositories and other deposit platforms. Re3data (https://www.re3data.org/) includes tags to show which platforms that it indexes assign PIDs to their content.

fieldSet.title: Persistent identifiers
fieldSet.description: A persistent identifier or PID is a long-lasting reference to a resource. Whatever resource it refers to, the primary purpose of the PID is to provide the information required to reliably identify, verify and locate it. A PID may be connected to a set of metadata describing an item rather than to the item itself.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Learn more about PIDs and how to use them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-can-identifiers-improve-the-dissemination-of-your-research-outputs

fieldSet.title: Will you provide searchable metadata for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Searchable metadata are metadata indexed by search engines and are identifiable by web crawlers.

selectData.label: Metadata are indexed in/ by a

fieldSet.title: What services will you use to provide searchable metadata?

labelData.label: URL of the services providing searchable metadata
Name of the services used for providing data with searchable metadata

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Name for the used searchable metadata
Will you use standardised formats for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Different types of data are acquired, processed and stored (preserved and/or archived) in different ways and can be discipline specific. Many proprietary file formats are “containers” for standard file formats. By packaging them into these containers, a software and/or hardware developer can provide additional functionality, usually by streamlining a process, to analyse data acquired on their platform. However, this has the negative consequence of making these data less interoperable.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find some best practices for file formats: https://library.stanford.edu/research/data-management-services/data-best-practices/best-practices-file-formats

labelData.label: URL where this information can be found

fieldSet.title: Provide information about used standardised formats
fieldSet.description: Is the structure of the file(s) provided in a standardised format?

fieldSet.title: Please describe the formats you plan to store the described data in, including any URLs to documentation.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information about preserving data in appropriate formats: https://www.openaire.eu/data-formats-preservation-guide

fieldSet.title: Are the file formats you will use open?
fieldSet.description: Open file formats provide the software specifications to anyone for free and enable re-use. Examples: .png, .xml, .rtf, .wav

fieldSet.title: Please provide more details if data are not in an open format.
fieldSet.description: Closed file formats are intertwined with proprietary formats that either do not provide access to software specification as opposed to open or despite the fact that they do, they limit re-use by others. Examples: .doc, .jpeg, .mp3

fieldSet.title: Do supported open-source tools exist for accessing the data?
fieldSet.description: Please provide the name and type of datasets that require the use of proprietary tools to be accessed.

fieldSet.title: Please describe if data require proprietary tools to access the data.
Will you provide metadata describing the quality of the data?

section.description: Data are findable when described with metadata and vocabularies in a standardized way, assigned a Persistent Identifiers (PIDs) and are registered or indexed in a searchable resource.
section.title: Making data findable, including provisions for metadata

fieldSet.title: Are there ethical or legal issues that can impact sharing the described data?
fieldSet.description: It is possible that data are sensitive, such as personal or confidential, and hence they can not be shared directly to others. For those types of data, specific processes need to be made in order to ensure their reliable exchange in the short and long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about sensitive data and how to process them: https://www.openaire.eu/sensitive-data-guide

fieldSet.title: Will the described data be openly accessible?

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: How will the data be made available?
fieldSet.description: A data repository, otherwise known as a data archive, is an online collection of datasets that are described and classified in a standard way that makes data discovery and retrieval easier to be performed by both humans and machines.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information on the processes, you can have a look at https://www.openaire.eu/rdm-handbook.

labelData.label: URL where the data are stored
Name of the repository that the data are stored

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Name of used data repositories

options.label: secure with no backup and recovery
insecure with no backup or recovery

fieldSet.title: Is the storage sufficiently secure for the data and does the storage provide backup and recovery procedures?
fieldSet.description: To learn more about best practices for back up and data security, you may have a look at: https://www.openaire.eu/raw-data-backup-and-versioning and https://www.uu.nl/en/research/research-data-management/guides/storing-and-preserving-data

fieldSet.title: Are there any methods or tools required to access the described data?
fieldSet.description: Describe processes needed to query and access data. These might be relevant to SPARQL access points, visualizer software, download, etc. Data access controls, such as passwords or firewalls, should be used to limit access to confidential data and protect them from unauthorised changes.

labelData.label: Briefly explain the method(s) need to be applied for data access
URL where the method can be found

referenceTypeData.label: Please select the tool from the list

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the tools needed to access the data.

selectData.label: Select your answer from the list

fieldSet.title: Will you also make auxiliary data that may be of interest to researchers available?
fieldSet.description: Auxiliary data are supplementary to the product generation data; data that come from an external source and not from direct instrument measures, but are used in the context of a study.

section.description: Not all data can be made publicly open, hence data can be FAIR but not open, or open but not FAIR or both FAIR and open. Data are accessible when uploaded in a data repository and retrieved by their PIDs. When data can not be shared openly, metadata should be provided (even when the data are no longer available). In the case of sensitive or personal data, anonymization or pseudonymization and specific access rights can be applied. Where accessing data requires the use of complementary methods or tools, such procedures should be documented.
section.title: Making data openly accessible

fieldSet.title: Will you use a controlled vocabulary for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Controlled vocabularies provide standard terminology as opposed to keywords or tags used to classify information. Examples: taxonomies, ontologies, thesauri

fieldSet.title: Will you provide a mapping to more commonly used ontologies?

section.description: Data are interoperable, meaning they can be easily understood and shared with other platforms and systems, when they are created using standard vocabularies and include references to other data and metadata.
section.title: Making data interoperable

selectData.label: Select from the list of answers

fieldSet.title: When do you plan to make thedescribed data available for reuse?
Please specify how long after the project has ended the data will be made available
fieldSet.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.

fieldSet.title: Please describe the reason the desribed data will not be made available
What internationally recognised licence(s) will you use for the described data?
fieldSet.description: There are a number of licenses that can be assigned to your research outputs. There are different licenses for software and for publications and data. For the latter, a common practice is the use of Creative Commons licenses which are machine readable.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
You may learn more about how to choose a license here: https://www.openaire.eu/how-do-i-license-my-research-data
Additionally, you may use the License Clearance Tool (LCT) to establish the proper open license required for the creation of a new (or synthetic) dataset, media, software, etc. or for the re-use of existing unlicensed content.. Access the tool here: https://lct.ni4os.eu/lct/login

fieldSet.title: Do you have documented procedures for quality assurance of the described data?
fieldSet.description: Quality assurance procedures are closely related to RDM and are meant to validate that data are clean (no duplicates or inconsistencies), error-free, well structured and represented in analysis. Quality assurance checks can be facilitated by the use of tools or scripts.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To familiarise yourself with the process, you may find a checklist here: https://www.eur.nl/sites/corporate/files/2017-11/Checklist_QA_QC_RDM_0_2_MD_20150129.pdf

labelData.label: URL where this information can be found

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL with the documented procedures

options.label: Set up of scientific and technical committee
options.value: Set up of scientific and technical committee

options.label: Use of tools for automatic checks
options.value: Use of tools for automatic checks

options.label: Consistency verified with data models and standards
options.value: Consistency verified with data models and standards

fieldSet.title: Describe the data quality assurance processes
Will you provide any support for data reuse?

selectData.label: Select from the list of answers

fieldSet.title: How long do you intend to support data reuse?

section.description: Data can be reused when the conditions about how others can make use of the data are well-described following community-standards and are communicated as specified by the owners. Such information can be found in licenses attributed to data and in references about the data provenance.
section.title: Increase data reuse

fieldSet.title: How will the cost of making the described data findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable be covered?
fieldSet.description: Research Data Management is not a costless procedure, so it is important that it is thought through at the planning stage of research.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

fieldSet.title: Will you identify a data manager to manage the described data? If not who will be responsible for the management of the data?

referenceTypeData.label: Search by using ORCIDs of people responsible for data management

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: Identify the people or roles that will be responsible for the management of the described data
fieldSet.description: Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project. Follow the research data management lifecycle to guide your steps in data capture, metadata production, data quality, storage and backup, data archiving, and data sharing.

fieldSet.title: How do you intend to ensure data reuse after your project finishes?
fieldSet.description: Despite where data are stored for everyday activities during the project lifetime, there are certain options to choose from data storage that ensures reuse in the log run. Most popular are data archives, otherwise found as repositories. Depending of data volume, archiving might be subject to small fees.

section.description: Data management can be costly, especially when its planning hasn't been sufficient from the very beginning of the research process. Costing of data management includes for example potential use of proprietary services and tools or extra effort needed to perform specific tasks or even to develop tools from scratch.
section.title: Allocation of resources

- https://zenodo.org/records/11066458

fieldSet.title: What is the purpose of the data collection/generation and its relation to the objectives of the project?
fieldSet.description: Data collection is usually at the beginning stages of research data management lifecycles to set the background of what is needed (data generation), what is already there (data reuse) and how to best use it to fulfill the project’s objectives (why). Here you may add information about the scope and objectives of your data collection process.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find a comparison of different data management lifecycles here: Plale, Beth & Kouper, Inna. (2017). The Centrality of Data: Data Lifecycle and Data Pipelines. 10.1016/B978-0-12-809715-1.00004-3.

fieldSet.description: The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. Secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. fieldSet.title: Persistent identifiers
fieldSet.description: A persistent identifier or PID is a long-lasting reference to a resource. Whatever resource it refers to, the primary purpose of the PID is to provide the information required to reliably identify, verify and locate it. A PID may be connected to a set of metadata describing an item rather than to the item itself.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Learn more about PIDs and how to use them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-can-identifiers-improve-the-dissemination-of-your-research-outputs

fieldSet.title: Will you provide searchable metadata for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Searchable metadata are metadata indexed by search engines and are identifiable by web crawlers.

selectData.label: Metadata are indexed in/ by a

fieldSet.title: What services will you use to provide searchable metadata?

labelData.label: URL of the services providing searchable metadata
Name of the services used for providing data with searchable metadata

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Name for the used searchable metadata
Will you use standardised formats for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Different types of data are acquired, processed and stored (preserved and/or archived) in different ways and can be discipline specific. Many proprietary file formats are “containers” for standard file formats. By packaging them into these containers, a software and/or hardware developer can provide additional functionality, usually by streamlining a process, to analyse data acquired on their platform. However, this has the negative consequence of making these data less interoperable.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find some best practices for file formats: https://library.stanford.edu/research/data-management-services/data-best-practices/best-practices-file-formats

labelData.label: URL where this information can be found

fieldSet.title: Provide information about used standardised formats
fieldSet.description: Is the structure of the file(s) provided in a standardised format?

fieldSet.title: Please describe the formats you plan to store the described data in, including any URLs to documentation.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information about preserving data in appropriate formats: https://www.openaire.eu/data-formats-preservation-guide

fieldSet.title: Are the file formats you will use open?
fieldSet.description: Open file formats provide the software specifications to anyone for free and enable re-use. Examples: .png, .xml, .rtf, .wav

fieldSet.title: Please provide more details if data are not in an open format.
fieldSet.description: Closed file formats are intertwined with proprietary formats that either do not provide access to software specification as opposed to open or despite the fact that they do, they limit re-use by others. Examples: .doc, .jpeg, .mp3

fieldSet.title: Do supported open-source tools exist for accessing the data?
fieldSet.description: Please provide the name and type of datasets that require the use of proprietary tools to be accessed.

fieldSet.title: Please describe if data require proprietary tools to access the data.
Will you provide metadata describing the quality of the data?

section.description: Data are findable when described with metadata and vocabularies in a standardized way, assigned a Persistent Identifiers (PIDs) and are registered or indexed in a searchable resource.
section.title: Making data findable, including provisions for metadata

fieldSet.title: Are there ethical or legal issues that can impact sharing the described data?
fieldSet.description: It is possible that data are sensitive, such as personal or confidential, and hence they can not be shared directly to others. For those types of data, specific processes need to be made in order to ensure their reliable exchange in the short and long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about sensitive data and how to process them: https://www.openaire.eu/sensitive-data-guide

fieldSet.title: Will the described data be openly accessible?

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: How will the data be made available?
fieldSet.description: A data repository, otherwise known as a data archive, is an online collection of datasets that are described and classified in a standard way that makes data discovery and retrieval easier to be performed by both humans and machines.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information on the processes, you can have a look at https://www.openaire.eu/rdm-handbook.

labelData.label: URL where the data are stored
Name of the repository that the data are stored

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Name of used data repositories

options.label: secure with no backup and recovery
insecure with no backup or recovery

fieldSet.title: Is the storage sufficiently secure for the data and does the storage provide backup and recovery procedures?
fieldSet.description: To learn more about best practices for back up and data security, you may have a look at: https://www.openaire.eu/raw-data-backup-and-versioning and https://www.uu.nl/en/research/research-data-management/guides/storing-and-preserving-data

fieldSet.title: Are there any methods or tools required to access the described data?
fieldSet.description: Describe processes needed to query and access data. These might be relevant to SPARQL access points, visualizer software, download, etc. Data access controls, such as passwords or firewalls, should be used to limit access to confidential data and protect them from unauthorised changes.

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the tools needed to access the data.

selectData.label: Select your answer from the list

fieldSet.title: Will you also make auxiliary data that may be of interest to researchers available?
fieldSet.description: Auxiliary data are supplementary to the product generation data; data that come from an external source and not from direct instrument measures, but are used in the context of a study.

section.description: Not all data can be made publicly open, hence data can be FAIR but not open, or open but not FAIR or both FAIR and open. Data are accessible when uploaded in a data repository and retrieved by their PIDs. When data can not be shared openly, metadata should be provided (even when the data are no longer available). In the case of sensitive or personal data, anonymization or pseudonymization and specific access rights can be applied. Where accessing data requires the use of complementary methods or tools, such procedures should be documented.
section.title: Making data openly accessible

fieldSet.title: Will you use a controlled vocabulary for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Controlled vocabularies provide standard terminology as opposed to keywords or tags used to classify information. Examples: taxonomies, ontologies, thesauri

fieldSet.title: Will you provide a mapping to more commonly used ontologies?

section.description: Data are interoperable, meaning they can be easily understood and shared with other platforms and systems, when they are created using standard vocabularies and include references to other data and metadata.
section.title: Making data interoperable

selectData.label: Select from the list of answers

fieldSet.title: When do you plan to make thedescribed data available for reuse?
Please specify how long after the project has ended the data will be made available
fieldSet.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.

fieldSet.title: Please describe the reason the desribed data will not be made available
What internationally recognised licence(s) will you use for the described data?
fieldSet.description: There are a number of licenses that can be assigned to your research outputs. There are different licenses for software and for publications and data. For the latter, a common practice is the use of Creative Commons licenses which are machine readable.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
You may learn more about how to choose a license here: https://www.openaire.eu/how-do-i-license-my-research-data
Additionally, you may use the License Clearance Tool (LCT) to establish the proper open license required for the creation of a new (or synthetic) dataset, media, software, etc. or for the re-use of existing unlicensed content.. Access the tool here: https://lct.ni4os.eu/lct/login

fieldSet.title: Do you have documented procedures for quality assurance of the described data?
fieldSet.description: Quality assurance procedures are closely related to RDM and are meant to validate that data are clean (no duplicates or inconsistencies), error-free, well structured and represented in analysis. Quality assurance checks can be facilitated by the use of tools or scripts.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To familiarise yourself with the process, you may find a checklist here: https://www.eur.nl/sites/corporate/files/2017-11/Checklist_QA_QC_RDM_0_2_MD_20150129.pdf

labelData.label: URL where this information can be found

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL with the documented procedures

options.value: Set up of scientific and technical committee

options.label: Use of tools for automatic checks
options.value: Use of tools for automatic checks

options.label: Consistency verified with data models and standards
options.value: Consistency verified with data models and standards

fieldSet.title: Describe the data quality assurance processes
Will you provide any support for data reuse?

selectData.label: Select from the list of answers

fieldSet.title: How long do you intend to support data reuse?

section.description: Data can be reused when the conditions about how others can make use of the data are well-described following community-standards and are communicated as specified by the owners. Such information can be found in licenses attributed to data and in references about the data provenance.
section.title: Increase data reuse

fieldSet.title: How will the cost of making the described data findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable be covered?
fieldSet.description: Research Data Management is not a costless procedure, so it is important that it is thought through at the planning stage of research.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

fieldSet.title: Will you identify a data manager to manage the described data? If not who will be responsible for the management of the data?

referenceTypeData.label: Search by using ORCIDs of people responsible for data management

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: Identify the people or roles that will be responsible for the management of the described data
fieldSet.description: Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project. Follow the research data management lifecycle to guide your steps in data capture, metadata production, data quality, storage and backup, data archiving, and data sharing.

fieldSet.title: How do you intend to ensure data reuse after your project finishes?
fieldSet.description: Despite where data are stored for everyday activities during the project lifetime, there are certain options to choose from data storage that ensures reuse in the log run. Most popular are data archives, otherwise found as repositories. Depending of data volume, archiving might be subject to small fees.

section.description: Data management can be costly, especially when its planning hasn't been sufficient from the very beginning of the research process. Costing of data management includes for example potential use of proprietary services and tools or extra effort needed to perform specific tasks or even to develop tools from scratch.
section.title: Allocation of resources

Metadata is indexed and searchable directly in Zenodo's search engine
immediately after publishing and sent to Datacite servers and indexed

description.description:
the dataset "Biodegradable mulching film degradation in soil in Italy" " and  the Image "Biodegradable mulching film degradation in soil in Italy" show the visible degradation process of biodegradable mulching samples in the soil at the experimental field of the University of Bari (Italy).

https://zenodo.org/records/11048438
description.label: SubTask 1.2.3 - Quantitative (tabular data) and qualitative (text) description of Mulching film degradation in different case studies in Europe.

fieldSet.title: What is the purpose of the data collection/generation and its relation to the objectives of the project?
fieldSet.description: Data collection is usually at the beginning stages of research data management lifecycles to set the background of what is needed (data generation), what is already there (data reuse) and how to best use it to fulfill the project’s objectives (why). Here you may add information about the scope and objectives of your data collection process.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find a comparison of different data management lifecycles here: Plale, Beth & Kouper, Inna. (2017). The Centrality of Data: Data Lifecycle and Data Pipelines. 10.1016/B978-0-12-809715-1.00004-3.

options.label: observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)
options.value: observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: What are the types of the described generated/collected data?
fieldSet.description: The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. Primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. Secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information, have a look at: https://data.library.virginia.edu/data-management/plan/format-types/

fieldSet.title: What are the formats of the described generated/collected data?
fieldSet.description: Data may be found in different formats. Usually, outputs of specific instruments/ services/ tools where data are generated and/ or processed have a default way of exposing their outputs following specific standards. Most common distinctions between formats are proprietary and non proprietary formats, because they may impact data access in the long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information about formats, have a look at: https://www.ukdataservice.ac.uk/manage-data/format/recommended-formats

fieldSet.title: What is the origin of the described data?
What is the expected size of the described data?
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

fieldSet.title: To whom might it be useful ('data utility')?
fieldSet.description: Data generated or reused in the project can be useful for a number of stakeholders and third parties. Think about the target audience of your research, but also about possible third parties who could further exploit this data even after the project ends.

section.description: Hint: This section is usually associated with detailed activities of the project. If you are writing the DMP at a primary stage of the research process (i.e. first 6 months of the research project), think about the main activities that will be performed within Work Packages (WPs) and break them down so as to fit into a research data management lifecycle. Add new dataset description to describe different datasets or types of datasets that will be produced or re-used during the project.
section.title: Data Summary

options.value: To compare and combine with other data

options.value: To follow-up research on a specific area

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using the described data and how?
fieldSet.description: Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc. 

Learn more: https://www.openaire.eu/can-i-reuse-someone-else-research-data

fieldSet.title: Where do the described data reside?
fieldSet.description: Select the repository/ies where data are hosted.

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: Which data will be re-used?
fieldSet.description: Select the datasets that will be reused. Make sure that you select the correct filenames and versions!

section.description: This section provides information about data that are re-used in the context of undertaking research.
section.title: Reused Data

section.description:
In general terms, your research data should be 'FAIR', that is findable, accessible, interoperable and re-usable. These principles precede implementation choices and do not necessarily suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution. This template is not intended as a strict technical implementation of the FAIR principles, it is rather inspired by FAIR as a general concept. Achieving FAIR data relies equally on the choices you make throughout a research process, but also on providers whose services enable FAIRness of data to be met. 
Learn more about the FAIR principles: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4792175/ 
Learn how you can comply with FAIR principles: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-make-your-data-fair
section.title: FAIR Data

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: Will you use metadata to describe the data?
fieldSet.description: Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. Descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its title, author/creator, abstract, keywords. Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

labelData.label: URL where metadata can be found
Name of repository, database, platform that holds the data

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Location describing the used metadata schema
Will your metadata use standardised vocabularies?
fieldSet.description: Standardised vocabularies enable greater interoperability across systems. There are generic and discipline specific metadata standards which have been compiled so as to contain vital information that enable exchange while taking into consideration specificities of work and demands in different disciplines, even in different areas of work within a discipline.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may browse the Metadata Standards Catalog here: http://rd-alliance.github.io/metadata-directory/

labelData.label: URL where the vocabularies of the data can be found
Briefly describe selected vocabularies application for your needs

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Description of used vocabularies
Will you make the metadata available free-of-charge?
fieldSet.description: You could offer your metadata records free of charge or assign them small fees. However, charging for metadata sharing would limit access and use. Please check your funder’s policy before deciding how and when to share metadata.

fieldSet.title: Will your metadata be harvestable?
fieldSet.description: Usually, this is facilitated by services that apply standard protocols for information exchange. For example, in repositories, metadata exchange is supported by the OAI-PMH protocol.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find more information about trustworthy repositories: https://www.openaire.eu/find-trustworthy-data-repository

fieldSet.title: Will you use naming conventions for your data?
fieldSet.description: Naming conventions used throughout the management process, such as file names or data entities, should be clearly described in order to make data understandable by other researchers who may reuse them after the project ends.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Check out some examples of file naming best practices: http://tiny.cc/op8lcz

labelData.label: URL where this information can be found
Name of the conventions used for the data

fieldSet.title: Please provide more details and examples on used naming conversions
Will you provide clear version numbers for your data?
fieldSet.description: Versioning may be a built-in functionality, subject to an instrument/service/tool that has been used for data processing and analysis which automatically produces versioned data. In other occasions, you will need to keep track and adapt to a versioning mechanism for your data. Here you should state how versioning takes place.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about how to handle and arrange different versions: https://www.openaire.eu/raw-data-backup-and-versioning

fieldSet.title: Will you provide persistent identifiers for the described data?
fieldSet.description: Persistent and/or Permanent Identifiers (PIDs) uniquely identify objects, people, organisations and activities and can ensure that the scientific output is accessible even when the URL of the website has changed. PIDs can be assigned to research outputs including publications, data and software/code. PIDs can also be assigned to researchers, samples, organisations and projects. A PID may be connected to a metadata record describing an item rather than the item itself. PIDs are usually provided by data repositories and other deposit platforms. Re3data (https://www.re3data.org/) includes tags to show which platforms that it indexes assign PIDs to their content.

fieldSet.title: Persistent identifiers
fieldSet.description: A persistent identifier or PID is a long-lasting reference to a resource. Whatever resource it refers to, the primary purpose of the PID is to provide the information required to reliably identify, verify and locate it. A PID may be connected to a set of metadata describing an item rather than to the item itself.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Learn more about PIDs and how to use them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-can-identifiers-improve-the-dissemination-of-your-research-outputs

fieldSet.title: How long do you intend to support data reuse?

section.description: Data can be reused when the conditions about how others can make use of the data are well-described following community-standards and are communicated as specified by the owners. Such information can be found in licenses attributed to data and in references about the data provenance.
section.title: Increase data reuse

fieldSet.title: How will the cost of making the described data findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable be covered?
fieldSet.description: Research Data Management is not a costless procedure, so it is important that it is thought through at the planning stage of research.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

fieldSet.title: Identify the people or roles that will be responsible for the management of the described data
fieldSet.description: Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project. Follow the research data management lifecycle to guide your steps in data capture, metadata production, data quality, storage and backup, data archiving, and data sharing.

fieldSet.title: How do you intend to ensure data reuse after your project finishes?
fieldSet.description: Despite where data are stored for everyday activities during the project lifetime, there are certain options to choose from data storage that ensures reuse in the log run. Most popular are data archives, otherwise found as repositories. Depending of data volume, archiving might be subject to small fees.

section.description: Data management can be costly, especially when its planning hasn't been sufficient from the very beginning of the research process. Costing of data management includes for example potential use of proprietary services and tools or extra effort needed to perform specific tasks or even to develop tools from scratch.
section.title: Allocation of resources

description.description:
Task: Transfer and behaviour of MNP to groundwater and surface water
Clusters of tabular data (with metadata including variable labels, code labels and definition of missing value, experimental design description) reporting estimators of the transport efficiency of micronanoplastics during infiltration through soils and breackthrough curves in column experiments under variable saturation conditions.

https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.10887824
https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.10932192
https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.10931958
description.label: SubTask 2.3.1 - MNP transport through soils under variable saturation conditions

fieldSet.title: What is the purpose of the data collection/generation and its relation to the objectives of the project?
fieldSet.description: Data collection is usually at the beginning stages of research data management lifecycles to set the background of what is needed (data generation), what is already there (data reuse) and how to best use it to fulfill the project’s objectives (why). Here you may add information about the scope and objectives of your data collection process.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find a comparison of different data management lifecycles here: Plale, Beth & Kouper, Inna. (2017). The Centrality of Data: Data Lifecycle and Data Pipelines. 10.1016/B978-0-12-809715-1.00004-3.

options.label: observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)
options.value: observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: What are the types of the described generated/collected data?
fieldSet.description: The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. Primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. Secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information, have a look at: https://data.library.virginia.edu/data-management/plan/format-types/

fieldSet.title: What are the formats of the described generated/collected data?
fieldSet.description: Data may be found in different formats. Usually, outputs of specific instruments/ services/ tools where data are generated and/ or processed have a default way of exposing their outputs following specific standards. Most common distinctions between formats are proprietary and non proprietary formats, because they may impact data access in the long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For more information about formats, have a look at: https://www.ukdataservice.ac.uk/manage-data/format/recommended-formats

fieldSet.title: What is the origin of the described data?
What is the expected size of the described data?
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

fieldSet.title: To whom might it be useful ('data utility')?
fieldSet.description: Data generated or reused in the project can be useful for a number of stakeholders and third parties. Think about the target audience of your research, but also about possible third parties who could further exploit this data even after the project ends.

section.description: Hint: This section is usually associated with detailed activities of the project. If you are writing the DMP at a primary stage of the research process (i.e. first 6 months of the research project), think about the main activities that will be performed within Work Packages (WPs) and break them down so as to fit into a research data management lifecycle. Add new dataset description to describe different datasets or types of datasets that will be produced or re-used during the project.
section.title: Data Summary

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using the described data and how?
fieldSet.description: Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context.
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc. 

Learn more: https://www.openaire.eu/can-i-reuse-someone-else-research-data
